metadata.purpose: Proof-of-concept dataset connecting biblical history, appointed times, biblical calendar dates, traditional chronology, historical events, and Gregorian alignment where known.
metadata.editorial_warning: This file intentionally preserves uncertainty. Traditional associations, historical reconstructions, and theological readings are labeled rather than flattened into fact claims.

entries.title: Creation Week

traditional_year.label: Year 1 from creation in traditional Jewish year counting
traditional_year.source_note: Traditional Jewish calendar reckoning counts years from creation; exact handling of year 1 and Adam's creation differs in technical chronology discussions.

entries.description: The biblical opening of ordered time, creation, blessing, and Sabbath rest.

traditions.name: Creation and Tishrei tradition
traditions.tradition: One rabbinic opinion associates creation with Tishrei; another tradition places creation in Nisan.
traditions.source_note: Babylonian Talmud, Rosh Hashanah 10b-11a discusses Tishrei and Nisan creation viewpoints.

viewpoints.label: Tishrei creation view
viewpoints.summary: Creation is associated with the autumn civil new year tradition.

viewpoints.label: Nisan creation view
viewpoints.summary: A competing rabbinic view associates creation with Nisan, the spring month of redemption.

source_notes.label: Genesis creation account
source_notes.note: Primary biblical text gives sequence but not a Gregorian date.

entries.notes: Keep creation chronology as theological and traditional, not as a forced modern date.

entries.title: Adam and Eve Created

hebrew_date.source_note: Common Jewish tradition associates the creation of humanity with Rosh Hashanah.

entries.description: The creation of humanity in the image of God. The association with 1 Tishrei is traditional, not an explicit biblical date.

traditions.name: Rosh Hashanah as birthday of humanity
traditions.tradition: Rosh Hashanah is commonly described in Jewish tradition as commemorating the creation of Adam and Eve or the beginning of humanity.
traditions.source_note: Tishrei/Rosh Hashanah tradition; see rabbinic discussions in Rosh Hashanah 10b-11a.

source_notes.label: Tishrei tradition summary
source_notes.note: Summarizes the tradition that 1 Tishrei is associated with Adam and Eve's creation according to one Talmudic opinion.

entries.notes: Useful for Today Card and Trumpets/Rosh Hashanah studies, but should be clearly marked as tradition.

entries.title: Fall in Eden
entries.description: The biblical account of human disobedience, exile from Eden, and the first promise of conflict between the serpent and the woman's seed.

source_notes.label: Genesis fall account
source_notes.note: No Hebrew or Gregorian date is given in the biblical text.

entries.notes: Do not invent a festival date for this entry unless owner chooses to track a specific tradition later.

entries.title: Flood Begins

hebrew_date.source_note: Genesis 7:11 gives the day and month number, but calendar starting point is debated.

traditional_year.label: Traditional year of the Flood
traditional_year.source_note: Common traditional chronology places the Flood in AM 1656.

entries.description: The floodwaters begin in Noah's six hundredth year, on the seventeenth day of the second month.

viewpoints.label: Nisan-based month numbering
viewpoints.summary: If the biblical month count is read from Nisan, the second month is Iyar.
viewpoints.source_note: Calendar-basis inference; Genesis gives month number, not the later month name.

viewpoints.label: Tishrei/civil-year reading
viewpoints.summary: Some traditional readings align the second month in the flood narrative with Cheshvan.
viewpoints.source_note: Depends on how pre-Exodus month numbering is handled.

source_notes.label: Genesis 7:11
source_notes.note: Primary biblical date: second month, seventeenth day.

entries.notes: This entry demonstrates why calendar_basis is needed.

entries.title: Ark Rests on the Mountains of Ararat

hebrew_date.source_note: Genesis 8:4 gives the day and month number.

entries.description: The ark comes to rest after the judgment waters, marking a movement toward renewed earth and covenant.

traditions.name: Possible Firstfruits resonance
traditions.tradition: Some biblical-theology readings notice a thematic resonance between the ark resting on a seventeenth day and later firstfruits/resurrection patterns, but this is not an explicit biblical appointment.
traditions.source_note: Interpretive pattern only; not entered as a firm date claim.

source_notes.label: Genesis 8:4
source_notes.note: Primary biblical date: seventh month, seventeenth day.

entries.notes: Keep this as a cautious thematic link unless owner wants typology fields expanded.

entries.title: Abraham Born - Traditional Chronology

traditional_year.label: Traditional year often assigned to Abraham's birth
traditional_year.source_note: Common Jewish traditional chronology derives patriarchal years from Genesis genealogies and Seder Olam-style reckoning.

entries.description: A traditional chronology marker for Abraham's life, useful as an anchor for patriarchal timeline work.

viewpoints.label: Traditional Jewish chronology
viewpoints.summary: Uses AM year labels derived from rabbinic chronology and biblical genealogical calculations.

viewpoints.label: Academic historical chronology
viewpoints.summary: Usually treats patriarchal dates as difficult to anchor to a precise Gregorian year.

source_notes.label: Seder Olam background
source_notes.note: Summarizes rabbinic AM chronology and notes differences from modern calendar handling.

entries.notes: This record is a chronology marker, not a claim of Gregorian precision.

entries.title: Isaac Born - Passover Tradition

hebrew_date.source_note: A Jewish tradition associates Isaac's birth with 15 Nisan.

entries.description: Isaac's birth fulfills the promised son narrative. The 15 Nisan association is traditional rather than explicit in Genesis.

traditions.name: Birth of Isaac on Passover
traditions.tradition: Traditional Jewish chronologies often place Isaac's birth on 15 Nisan.
traditions.source_note: Nisan history summaries and rabbinic tradition preserve this association.

source_notes.label: Nisan traditional events
source_notes.note: Lists 15 Nisan tradition for Isaac's birth as a Jewish history/tradition entry.

entries.notes: Good example of a patriarchal event tied to an appointed time by tradition, not by explicit biblical date.

entries.title: Binding of Isaac - Rosh Hashanah Reading Association

hebrew_date.source_note: The Akedah is traditionally read on the second day of Rosh Hashanah; this is liturgical association, not an explicit event date.

entries.description: The binding of Isaac is deeply connected to Rosh Hashanah worship through synagogue reading and shofar themes, though Genesis does not date the event.

traditions.name: Akedah in Rosh Hashanah worship
traditions.tradition: Genesis 22 is traditionally read in synagogue on Rosh Hashanah, linking the ram and shofar imagery with the day.
traditions.source_note: Jewish liturgical practice; commonly noted in discussions of the Binding of Isaac.

source_notes.label: Binding of Isaac worship note
source_notes.note: Summarizes that Genesis 22 is traditionally read on the second day of Rosh Hashanah.

entries.notes: Store as liturgical/thematic association, not historical date.

entries.title: Passover

hebrew_date.display: 14 Nisan at twilight; meal begins 15 Nisan
hebrew_date.source_note: Exodus 12 and Leviticus 23 assign Passover to the first month, fourteenth day, at twilight.

entries.description: The appointed time remembering the LORD's deliverance of Israel from Egypt through the Passover lamb and the sparing of the firstborn.

viewpoints.label: Biblical wording
viewpoints.summary: Passover sacrifice is tied to 14 Nisan; the festival meal is eaten as the 15th begins.

source_notes.label: Passover overview
source_notes.note: Useful secondary overview of Passover date, biblical narrative, and Omer connection.

entries.notes: Future schema may separate sacrifice, seder/meal, and week of Unleavened Bread.

entries.title: The Exodus from Egypt

hebrew_date.source_note: The departure follows the Passover night; Numbers 33:3 states Israel set out on the fifteenth day of the first month.

traditional_year.label: Traditional Exodus year
traditional_year.source_note: Seder Olam-style Jewish chronology commonly labels the Exodus as AM 2448.

entries.description: Israel leaves Egypt after the Passover judgment, beginning the journey toward Sinai and covenant.

viewpoints.label: Traditional AM chronology
viewpoints.summary: Labels the Exodus as AM 2448.

viewpoints.label: Historical Exodus dating
viewpoints.summary: Academic and confessional chronologies vary; this proof of concept does not choose between early and late Exodus models.
viewpoints.source_note: Requires owner review before Gregorian alignment.

source_notes.label: Seder Olam background
source_notes.note: Summarizes the traditional AM chronology and notes AM 2448 for the Exodus in Seder Olam reckoning.

entries.notes: Do not add a Gregorian year until the app chooses how to handle early/late Exodus chronologies.

entries.title: Crossing of the Sea - Seventh Day Tradition

hebrew_date.source_note: Jewish tradition commonly associates the sea crossing with the seventh day of Unleavened Bread.

traditional_year.label: Traditional Exodus year
traditional_year.source_note: Tied to the traditional Exodus chronology.

entries.description: The crossing of the sea and the song of deliverance are traditionally connected with the seventh day of Unleavened Bread.

traditions.name: Seventh day sea crossing
traditions.tradition: The seventh day of Passover/Unleavened Bread is traditionally associated with the crossing of the sea.
traditions.source_note: Traditional Jewish holiday association; verify primary rabbinic reference in full dataset phase.

source_notes.label: Needs primary-source follow-up
source_notes.note: This association should be traced to primary rabbinic or liturgical sources before production use.

entries.notes: Marked traditional until source review is completed.

entries.title: Shavuot / Feast of Weeks

hebrew_date.display: 6 Sivan in the fixed rabbinic calendar
hebrew_date.source_note: Leviticus 23 gives a count from Firstfruits/Passover season; fixed Jewish observance places Shavuot on 6 Sivan.

entries.description: The Feast of Weeks marks the completion of the Omer count and is traditionally associated with the giving of the Torah at Sinai.

traditions.name: Giving of Torah
traditions.tradition: Rabbinic tradition associates Shavuot with the revelation at Sinai, though the Torah does not explicitly name Shavuot as the date of the giving of the Torah.
traditions.source_note: Shavuot tradition and Talmudic discussion preserve 6/7 Sivan viewpoints.

viewpoints.label: 6 Sivan view
viewpoints.summary: Most Talmudic sages associate the giving of Torah with 6 Sivan.

viewpoints.label: 7 Sivan view
viewpoints.summary: Rabbi Yose's view places the giving of Torah on 7 Sivan.
viewpoints.source_note: Talmudic discussion summarized in Shavuot references.

source_notes.label: Shavuot overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes biblical harvest meaning, rabbinic Sinai association, and 6/7 Sivan dispute.

entries.notes: Important model case for preserving a fixed observance, a biblical count, and a traditional historical association.

entries.title: Torah Given at Sinai

hebrew_date.display: 6 Sivan in common tradition; 7 Sivan in Rabbi Yose's view
hebrew_date.source_note: Talmudic discussion preserves more than one view.

traditional_year.label: Traditional Sinai year
traditional_year.source_note: Tied to the traditional Exodus year.

entries.description: The covenant revelation at Sinai is traditionally linked to Shavuot, with preserved discussion over the exact date.

traditions.name: Sinai and Shavuot
traditions.tradition: Shavuot is liturgically and traditionally called the season of the giving of the Torah.
traditions.source_note: Common Jewish liturgy and rabbinic tradition.

viewpoints.label: Common Shavuot alignment
viewpoints.summary: Torah given on 6 Sivan.

viewpoints.label: Rabbi Yose alignment
viewpoints.summary: Torah given on 7 Sivan.

source_notes.label: Shavuot and Sinai
source_notes.note: Notes that the Torah does not explicitly date the giving of Torah to Shavuot and summarizes rabbinic views.

entries.notes: Do not collapse 6 and 7 Sivan into a single unqualified fact.

entries.title: Tablets Broken after the Golden Calf

hebrew_date.source_note: Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6 lists the breaking of the tablets among calamities of 17 Tammuz.

entries.description: Moses breaks the tablets after the golden calf. Tradition connects this event with the fast of the seventeenth of Tammuz.

traditions.name: Five calamities of 17 Tammuz
traditions.tradition: The breaking of the tablets is one of the calamities traditionally assigned to 17 Tammuz.
traditions.source_note: Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6.

source_notes.label: Seventeenth of Tammuz overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6 and later fast-day traditions.

entries.notes: Good entry for linking fast-day calendar to Sinai narrative.

entries.title: Second Tablets and Forgiveness - Yom Kippur Tradition

hebrew_date.source_note: Jewish tradition connects Moses' return with the second tablets and divine forgiveness on Yom Kippur.

entries.description: Tradition connects Yom Kippur with Moses returning from Sinai with the second tablets and a message of forgiveness after the golden calf.

traditions.name: Second tablets on Yom Kippur
traditions.tradition: Moses' return with the second tablets is traditionally associated with 10 Tishrei.
traditions.source_note: Common Jewish tradition; primary rabbinic source should be linked in a later pass.

source_notes.label: Needs primary-source follow-up
source_notes.note: The association is widely taught but should be traced to primary rabbinic citations before production use.

entries.notes: This entry should not replace Leviticus 16 as the primary biblical basis for Yom Kippur.

entries.title: Spies' Report and Wilderness Judgment

hebrew_date.source_note: Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6 lists the decree after the spies among calamities of 9 Av.

entries.description: The negative report of the spies and Israel's refusal to enter the land are traditionally connected with Tisha B'Av.

traditions.name: First calamity of 9 Av
traditions.tradition: The spies' report and resulting wilderness judgment are listed among the calamities of 9 Av.
traditions.source_note: Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6.

source_notes.label: Tisha B'Av overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6 and the traditional five calamities of 9 Av.

entries.notes: Useful for connecting wilderness narrative to later Temple mourning.

entries.title: Israel Crosses the Jordan into the Land

hebrew_date.source_note: Joshua 4:19 gives the tenth day of the first month.

entries.description: Israel crosses the Jordan and camps at Gilgal on the tenth day of the first month.

source_notes.label: Joshua 4:19
source_notes.note: Primary biblical date.

entries.notes: Potential visual anchor for crossing/map timeline.

entries.title: First Passover in the Land

hebrew_date.source_note: Joshua 5:10 gives the fourteenth day of the month.

entries.description: Israel keeps Passover at Gilgal; the manna ceases after they eat from the produce of the land.

source_notes.label: Joshua 5:10-12
source_notes.note: Primary biblical text links Passover, land entry, and eating the produce of Canaan.

entries.notes: This entry bridges Calendar Card, land studies, and harvest themes.

entries.title: Solomon's Temple Dedication and Sukkot Season

hebrew_date.display: Seventh month, during a fourteen-day assembly
hebrew_date.source_note: 1 Kings 8 places the assembly in Ethanim, the seventh month; the exact starting day is interpreted from the dedication sequence.

entries.description: Solomon gathers Israel in the seventh month for the dedication of the Temple, with the celebration overlapping the Sukkot season.

traditions.name: Temple dedication and Sukkot
traditions.tradition: The dedication is associated with the feast season in the seventh month, especially Sukkot.
traditions.source_note: 1 Kings 8:2, 65-66 and 2 Chronicles 7:8-10 connect the dedication with the seventh-month feast period.

source_notes.label: Biblical dedication texts
source_notes.note: Primary texts give the seventh month and feast context.

entries.notes: Later pass can add traditional date labels such as 8 Tishrei if owner approves source handling.

entries.title: Babylonian Siege of Jerusalem Begins

hebrew_date.source_note: 2 Kings 25:1 and Ezekiel 24:1-2 give the tenth day of the tenth month.

gregorian_date.source_note: Historical chronology varies slightly; the fast date remains fixed as 10 Tevet.

entries.description: Nebuchadnezzar's siege of Jerusalem begins, later remembered in Jewish fasting as the fast of the tenth month.

source_notes.label: Tenth of Tevet overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes biblical basis and later fast observance.

entries.notes: Good entry for linking prophetic texts to fast-day cycle.

entries.title: First Temple Destroyed

hebrew_date.source_note: 2 Kings 25:8 and Jeremiah 52:12 differ in wording/date emphasis; rabbinic tradition centers mourning on 9 Av.

traditional_year.label: Traditional Jewish year for First Temple destruction
traditional_year.source_note: Traditional Jewish chronology often gives 3338 AM / 423-422 BCE.

gregorian_date.display: 586 or 587 BCE in many modern historical chronologies
gregorian_date.source_note: Modern chronologies often place the destruction in 586 or 587 BCE; traditional Jewish chronology gives a later BCE label.

entries.description: The destruction of Solomon's Temple and the deepening of Judah's exile. The biblical texts, rabbinic fast date, traditional chronology, and modern historical chronology must be kept distinct.

traditions.name: Tisha B'Av mourning
traditions.tradition: The First Temple destruction is one of the central calamities mourned on Tisha B'Av.
traditions.source_note: Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6 and later Jewish practice.

viewpoints.label: Traditional Jewish chronology
viewpoints.summary: Often gives 3338 AM, approximately 423/422 BCE.
viewpoints.source_note: Traditional chronology differs from modern academic chronology.

viewpoints.label: Modern historical chronology
viewpoints.summary: Often gives 586 or 587 BCE.

source_notes.label: Tisha B'Av overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes Temple destruction commemoration and biblical/rabbinic date issues.

entries.notes: This is a key stress-test entry for multiple chronology systems.

entries.title: Return under Cyrus

gregorian_date.source_note: Common historical date for Cyrus's decree after the Persian conquest of Babylon; exact biblical-calendar day not provided.

entries.description: Cyrus permits the return and the rebuilding of the house of the LORD in Jerusalem.

source_notes.label: Ezra return texts
source_notes.note: The biblical text does not give a Hebrew calendar day for the decree.

entries.notes: Gregorian year should be reviewed against chosen historical chronology source in full phase.

entries.title: Second Temple Completed

hebrew_date.source_note: Ezra 6:15 gives the third day of Adar in the sixth year of Darius.

gregorian_date.source_note: Common historical alignment for Darius I's sixth year.

entries.description: The returned community completes the Second Temple and dedicates it with offerings.

source_notes.label: Ezra 6:15
source_notes.note: Primary biblical date.

entries.notes: Good example where biblical date and approximate historical year can coexist.

entries.title: Messiah's Death and Passover Season

hebrew_date.day: 14 or 15, depending on Gospel chronology interpretation
hebrew_date.source_note: The Passion narratives clearly place the death of Jesus/Yeshua in Passover season; exact calendrical harmonization is debated.

gregorian_date.source_note: Common reconstruction options; this dataset does not choose one in the first pass.

entries.description: The New Testament places Messiah's death in the Passover season and interprets him through Passover lamb imagery. Exact calendrical alignment requires careful handling.

traditions.name: Messiah as Passover
traditions.tradition: Christian and Messianic readings connect Messiah's death with Passover deliverance and lamb imagery.
traditions.source_note: 1 Corinthians 5:7 explicitly uses Passover language; chronology details remain debated.

viewpoints.label: Synoptic chronology emphasis
viewpoints.summary: The meal is presented with Passover meal language.
viewpoints.source_note: Matthew, Mark, and Luke require careful comparison.

viewpoints.label: John chronology emphasis
viewpoints.summary: John's timing is often read as aligning the death with preparation/passover lamb themes.

source_notes.label: Passover overview
source_notes.note: Background for Passover date and sacrifice; New Testament chronology needs dedicated sources in later phase.

entries.notes: Owner should decide how to represent Gospel chronology without forcing harmonization.

entries.title: Firstfruits / Wave Sheaf

hebrew_date.day: During Unleavened Bread; commonly 16 Nisan in rabbinic reckoning
hebrew_date.display: Omer begins 16 Nisan in rabbinic practice
hebrew_date.source_note: Leviticus 23 says the day after the Sabbath; later traditions differ on how Sabbath is read.

entries.description: The first sheaf offering begins the harvest count toward Shavuot. Later Jewish and Christian readings differ in calendrical and theological emphasis.

traditions.name: Rabbinic Omer start
traditions.tradition: Rabbinic Judaism begins the Omer on 16 Nisan, the second night of Passover.
traditions.source_note: Standard Jewish practice and Passover/Omer descriptions.

traditions.name: Resurrection firstfruits reading
traditions.tradition: Christian and Messianic readings often connect Messiah's resurrection with firstfruits language.
traditions.source_note: 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 calls Messiah the firstfruits of those who sleep.

viewpoints.label: Rabbinic reading
viewpoints.summary: The Sabbath in Leviticus 23 is read in relation to the festival, yielding a fixed 16 Nisan Omer start.

viewpoints.label: Weekly Sabbath reading
viewpoints.summary: Some groups read the Sabbath as the weekly Sabbath during Unleavened Bread, producing a Sunday Firstfruits count.
viewpoints.source_note: Preserved in non-rabbinic and some Christian/Messianic readings.

source_notes.label: Passover Omer overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes counting the Omer from 16 Nisan in Jewish practice.

source_notes.label: First Fruits overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes biblical firstfruits and New Testament firstfruits language.

entries.notes: This entry needs careful UI support for multiple calendar traditions.

entries.title: Messiah's Resurrection and Firstfruits Association

hebrew_date.day: During Unleavened Bread / first day of the week after the crucifixion
hebrew_date.display: First day of the week; Firstfruits association in many readings
hebrew_date.source_note: The Gospels state the resurrection was discovered on the first day of the week; Firstfruits linkage is theological and calendrical interpretation.

gregorian_date.display: Often tied to 30 or 33 CE Passion chronologies

entries.description: The New Testament witnesses the resurrection on the first day of the week and calls Messiah the firstfruits. The exact Hebrew date depends on Passion chronology and Firstfruits calendar interpretation.

traditions.name: Resurrection as Firstfruits
traditions.tradition: Christian and Messianic interpretation commonly associates the resurrection with Firstfruits based on 1 Corinthians 15.
traditions.source_note: 1 Corinthians 15:20-23 gives the firstfruits language; calendar alignment must be handled separately.

source_notes.label: First Fruits overview
source_notes.note: Notes 1 Corinthians' use of firstfruits language for Jesus' resurrection.

entries.notes: Do not present a single Nisan date until owner chooses chronology model.

entries.title: Yom Teruah / Rosh Hashanah

hebrew_date.source_note: Leviticus 23 gives a memorial of blowing on the first day of the seventh month.

entries.description: The biblical day of trumpet/shofar blowing on the first day of the seventh month, later observed as Rosh Hashanah in rabbinic Judaism.

traditions.name: Creation/humanity association
traditions.tradition: Rosh Hashanah is traditionally associated with creation, especially the creation of humanity.
traditions.source_note: Rabbinic Tishrei creation tradition.

viewpoints.label: Biblical festival name
viewpoints.summary: The Torah emphasizes a day of blowing/remembrance on 1 Tishrei.

viewpoints.label: Rabbinic New Year observance
viewpoints.summary: Rabbinic Judaism observes 1 Tishrei as Rosh Hashanah, the civil new year and day of judgment.
viewpoints.source_note: Mishnah Rosh Hashanah and later Jewish practice.

source_notes.label: Rosh Hashanah overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes biblical and rabbinic meanings.

entries.notes: Use both names where helpful; avoid erasing the biblical Yom Teruah language.

entries.title: Yom Kippur / Day of Atonement

hebrew_date.source_note: Leviticus 16 and 23 assign the Day of Atonement to the tenth day of the seventh month.

entries.description: The appointed day of affliction, atonement, and sanctuary cleansing in the seventh month.

traditions.name: Second tablets and forgiveness
traditions.tradition: Tradition associates Yom Kippur with Moses' return with the second tablets after the golden calf.
traditions.source_note: Common Jewish teaching; needs primary citation review before production.

source_notes.label: Leviticus 16 and 23
source_notes.note: Primary biblical basis for the day.

entries.notes: Keep the appointed time itself separate from later associated Sinai tradition.

entries.title: Sukkot / Feast of Tabernacles

hebrew_date.source_note: Leviticus 23 gives the fifteenth day of the seventh month and a seven-day feast.

entries.description: The seventh-month feast of booths, remembering wilderness dwelling and celebrating harvest joy before the LORD.

source_notes.label: Leviticus 23
source_notes.note: Primary biblical date and command.

entries.notes: Future expansion can add Hoshana Rabbah, Shemini Atzeret, water-pouring tradition, and Temple-era practices.

entries.title: Second Temple Destroyed

hebrew_date.source_note: The destruction of the Second Temple is commemorated on Tisha B'Av.

traditional_year.label: Traditional Jewish year sometimes given for Second Temple destruction
traditional_year.source_note: Rabbinic chronology differs from standard historical CE dating.

gregorian_date.display: 70 CE; exact Julian/Gregorian day conversion varies in summaries
gregorian_date.source_note: Standard historical date for Roman destruction of Jerusalem and the Temple.

entries.description: The Roman destruction of the Second Temple, remembered with the First Temple destruction on Tisha B'Av.

traditions.name: Tisha B'Av mourning
traditions.tradition: The Second Temple destruction is one of the central calamities mourned on Tisha B'Av.
traditions.source_note: Mishnah Ta'anit 4:6 and later Jewish practice.

viewpoints.label: Historical chronology
viewpoints.summary: Commonly dated to 70 CE.

viewpoints.label: Traditional rabbinic chronology
viewpoints.summary: Some rabbinic chronology labels differ by two years or more from standard historical summaries.
viewpoints.source_note: See Second Temple and Seder Olam chronology notes.

source_notes.label: Tisha B'Av overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes commemoration of First and Second Temple destructions.

source_notes.label: Second Temple overview
source_notes.note: Summarizes Second Temple chronology and rabbinic chronology differences.

entries.notes: Keep Jewish fast date, historical CE date, and traditional AM year as separate fields.
